---
title: "Cinderhaven Provisions · Product Data Readiness"
subtitle: "Executive Tearsheet · May 2026"
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: lualatex
    documentclass: scrartcl
    classoption: ["letterpaper"]
    geometry:
      - left=0.55in
      - right=0.55in
      - top=0.55in
      - bottom=0.55in
    fontsize: 9pt
    fig-align: center
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: chnavy
    urlcolor: chnavy
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{xcolor}
        % Cinderhaven palette (R/00_theme.R)
        \definecolor{chnavy}{HTML}{1B2A4A}
        \definecolor{chred}{HTML}{C0221F}
        \definecolor{chteal}{HTML}{1E8C7E}
        \definecolor{chcoral}{HTML}{D35830}
        \definecolor{chbgpale}{HTML}{E8ECF0}
        \definecolor{chtextmuted}{HTML}{636E72}
        \definecolor{chtext}{HTML}{2D3436}

        % Tight typography for a dense 2-page tearsheet.
        \usepackage{parskip}
        \setlength{\parskip}{2.4pt plus 0.5pt}
        \setlength{\parindent}{0pt}

        % KOMA section heading colors and tighter spacing above/below.
        \usepackage[automark]{scrlayer-scrpage}
        \setkomafont{section}{\color{chnavy}\large\bfseries}
        \setkomafont{subsection}{\color{chnavy}\normalsize\bfseries}
        \setkomafont{disposition}{\bfseries}
        \RedeclareSectionCommand[
          beforeskip=0.6\baselineskip,
          afterskip=0.15\baselineskip
        ]{section}
        \RedeclareSectionCommand[
          beforeskip=0.5\baselineskip,
          afterskip=0.1\baselineskip
        ]{subsection}

        % Title block tightening.
        \setkomafont{title}{\color{chnavy}\Large\bfseries}
        \setkomafont{subtitle}{\color{chtextmuted}\normalsize\itshape}
        \makeatletter
        \renewcommand*{\@maketitle}{%
          \par\vspace{-1.6em}%
          {\usekomafont{title}\@title\par}%
          \ifx\@subtitle\@empty\else
            \vspace{0.2em}%
            {\usekomafont{subtitle}\@subtitle\par}%
          \fi
          \vspace{0.2em}%
          {\color{chnavy}\rule{\linewidth}{0.6pt}}%
          \par\vspace{0.4em}%
        }
        \makeatother

        % Booktabs + table tweaks for the triage list.
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{array}
        \usepackage{colortbl}
        \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.06}

        % Compact lists.
        \usepackage{enumitem}
        \setlist{topsep=1pt, partopsep=0pt, itemsep=1pt, parsep=0pt,
                 leftmargin=*, labelsep=0.4em}

        % A "stat" macro for the page-1 lead numbers (red, bold, no break).
        \newcommand{\stat}[1]{\textbf{\color{chred}#1}}
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    out.width: "100%"
---

```{r setup}
#| include: false
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(dplyr); library(knitr); library(scales)
})
smf <- readRDS("../output/frames/sku_master_full.rds")
rrs <- readRDS("../output/frames/retailer_readiness_summary.rds")
cbe <- readRDS("../output/frames/chargebacks_enriched.rds")

n_skus <- nrow(smf)
total_cb_36mo <- sum(smf$chargeback_total, na.rm = TRUE)
annual_cb <- round(total_cb_36mo * 12 / 36)
total_ttm_rev <- sum(smf$ttm_revenue, na.rm = TRUE)

wm_readiness <- rrs |> filter(retailer == "Walmart") |>
  summarise(n_fail = sum(!overall_pass), n_total = n(),
            pass_rate = mean(overall_pass))
wm_fail <- wm_readiness$n_fail[1]
wm_rev_at_risk <- smf |>
  semi_join(rrs |> filter(retailer == "Walmart", !overall_pass),
            by = "sku") |>
  summarise(rev = sum(ttm_revenue, na.rm = TRUE)) |> pull(rev)
wm_rev_pct <- round(100 * wm_rev_at_risk / total_ttm_rev)

n_gtin_fail <- sum(!smf$gtin_valid, na.rm = TRUE)

ds <- function(x) {
  if (abs(x) >= 1e6) sprintf("%.1f million", x / 1e6)
  else formatC(round(x), big.mark = ",", format = "d")
}

stage3_skus <- n_skus * 5L
stage3_retailers <- 8L
stage3_cb <- round(annual_cb * (stage3_skus / n_skus) * (stage3_retailers / 3))

total_fix_hours <- sum(smf$est_fix_hours, na.rm = TRUE)
```

## The price of poor housekeeping

\stat{\$`r ds(annual_cb)` per year} in retailer chargebacks from product data defects, plus stalled-launch revenue loss and shelf loss from deauthorizations linked to data quality.

\stat{\$`r ds(wm_rev_at_risk)` in revenue at risk} at Walmart. `r wm_fail` of `r n_skus` SKUs fail Walmart's required-field check today. Those `r wm_fail` carry `r wm_rev_pct`% of the catalog's trailing twelve-month revenue. Costco and Whole Foods have similar gaps at lower thresholds.

\stat{\$`r ds(stage3_cb)` in projected annual chargebacks} at the growth target, if current defect rates hold. Each new SKU and each new retailer adds to the chargeback surface independently. The projection is linear. In practice, defect rates tend to worsen during rapid growth because data entry processes that strain at `r n_skus` SKUs break at `r n_skus * 5`.

## The compliance deadline

GS1 Sunrise 2027 transitions American retail barcodes to 2D codes built on validated GTINs. FSMA Rule 204 makes accurate GTINs a federal food traceability requirement. `r n_gtin_fail` of `r n_skus` SKUs carry GTINs that fail algorithmic validation today. Both deadlines are already in motion. A company that cannot pass a check digit calculation on its barcodes cannot participate in either transition.

## Summary

The defining feature of this problem is the asymmetry between cost and fix. Correcting every data defect that generates chargebacks takes `r round(total_fix_hours)` hours of data entry. The defects have been present for months. The connection between the defects and the charges has never been visible until this audit.

::: {layout-ncol=3}
![Chargeback Pareto](../output/charts/01_chargeback_pareto.png){fig-alt="Pareto curve: 5 SKUs drive 50% of chargeback dollars; 15 drive 80%."}

![Revenue at risk by retailer](../output/charts/06_retailer_readiness_revenue_at_risk.png){fig-alt="Horizontal bar chart of TTM revenue at risk per retailer."}

![Growth projection of chargebacks](../output/charts/12_growth_projection_chargebacks.png){fig-alt="Projected annual chargebacks at current scale, Stage 2, and Stage 3."}
:::

\newpage

## Top 10 by fix priority

```{r triage-table}
#| results: asis

fmt_dollar <- function(v) {
  ifelse(is.na(v), "—",
   ifelse(v >= 1000,
          paste0("\\$", formatC(round(v), big.mark = ",", format = "d")),
          paste0("\\$", formatC(round(v), big.mark = ",", format = "d"))))
}
fmt_per_hr <- function(v) {
  ifelse(is.na(v), "—",
         paste0("\\$", formatC(round(v), big.mark = ",", format = "d"), "/hr"))
}
fmt_minutes <- function(hr) {
  m <- round(hr * 60)
  ifelse(is.na(m) | m == 0, "—", paste0(m, " min"))
}
# Escape LaTeX special chars in product names (we keep escape=FALSE on
# kable so our pre-formatted dollar strings render correctly).
latex_escape <- function(x) {
  x <- gsub("\\\\", "\\\\textbackslash{}", x)
  x <- gsub("([&%$#_{}])", "\\\\\\1", x)
  x
}

triage <- smf |>
  arrange(desc(fix_priority_score)) |>
  slice(1:10) |>
  transmute(
    SKU = sku,
    Product = latex_escape(product_name),
    `Annual cb savings` = fmt_dollar(chargeback_total * 12 / 36),
    `Fix time` = fmt_minutes(est_fix_hours),
    `Savings per hour` = fmt_per_hr(savings_per_hour))

# Header colored navy with white text; rows zebra-striped via colortbl.
cat("\\rowcolors{2}{chbgpale!40}{white}\n")
cat(kable(triage, format = "latex", booktabs = TRUE,
          escape = FALSE,
          align = c("l", "l", "r", "r", "r"),
          linesep = ""),
    "\n")
```

## Three root causes

**No validation at entry.** The product master has no recorded entry source for any SKU. Nobody knows who entered these records or what process was followed. There is no validation gate between data entry and the live product master.

**Nearly every SKU carries the same defect.** `r n_gtin_fail` of `r n_skus` SKUs carry invalid GTIN-14 check digits. `r sum(!smf$upc_valid)` carry invalid UPC check digits. No SKU has both valid. All other fields — brand owner, case dimensions, country of origin — are complete and correct. The data quality completeness score is `r round(mean(smf$data_quality_score, na.rm = TRUE), 1)` for every SKU, but completeness masks the check-digit failures that retailers actually test.

**Margin density does not follow gross revenue.** Walmart is the largest retailer by gross dollars but not the most profitable by net margin percentage. Trade spend is negotiated annually. Chargebacks are generated by data defects that Cinderhaven can fix any Tuesday afternoon. Every dollar recovered drops straight to net contribution.

## The crown jewel

```{r tearsheet-top-rev}
#| include: false
top_rev <- smf |> dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(ttm_revenue)) |> dplyr::slice(1)
top_rev_events <- nrow(cbe[cbe$sku == top_rev$sku, ])
```

`r top_rev$sku`, `r top_rev$product_name`, is the highest-revenue SKU in the catalog (\$`r formatC(round(top_rev$ttm_revenue), big.mark = ",", format = "d")`) and among the largest sources of chargeback cost (\$`r formatC(round(top_rev$chargeback_total), big.mark = ",", format = "d")` over 36 months). `r top_rev_events` chargeback events across multiple retailers, driven by invalid barcode check digits. It fails most retailers' readiness checks. Fixing the barcodes takes `r round(top_rev$est_fix_hours * 60)` minutes.

## A one-week turnaround

- **Days 1–2:** Correct all barcode check digits (UPC and GTIN-14) across `r n_skus` SKUs. About `r round(sum(smf$est_fix_hours, na.rm = TRUE))` hours total. This addresses the entire data-defect scope and brings all SKUs to passing status at all retailers.
- **Days 3–4:** Implement a check-digit validation gate on every data entry path. The GS1 algorithm is a single modulo operation — block saves when the digit is wrong.
- **Day 5:** Deploy the Monday Morning Dashboard. Establish a monthly 30-minute data quality review.
- **Result:** The product master supports GS1 Sunrise 2027 compliance, FSMA Rule 204 traceability, and retailer expansion without compounding data debt.
